q: What are the three components of the cardiovascular system?
a: 1. Heart (pump) 2. Blood (fluid) 3. Blood vessels: Arteries, Veins, Capillaries

q: What are the three overarching functions of the cardiovascular system? (Table 14.1)
a: (1) Transport TO cells: delivers O2, nutrients, hormones, and immune cells/proteins to all body cells. (2) Remove FROM cells: carries metabolic wastes (CO2, heat, nitrogenous wastes) to excretory organs (lungs, kidneys, skin). (3) Cell-to-cell distribution: moves hormones from endocrine glands to target cells, glucose/fatty acids from storage to active cells, and immune cells/antibodies throughout the body.

q: Using Table 14.1, what materials ENTER the body via the cardiovascular system, and from where?
a: (1) Oxygen: absorbed in lungs → delivered to all cells. (2) Nutrients and water: absorbed from intestinal tract → delivered to all cells.

q: What materials does the cardiovascular system transport BETWEEN cells?
a: Hormones (endocrine cells to target cells), immune cells/antibodies/clotting proteins (continuously present in blood), and stored nutrients (from liver and adipose tissue to all cells).

q: Using Table 14.1, what materials LEAVE the body via the cardiovascular system, and to where?
a: (1) Carbon dioxide: from all cells → to lungs (exhaled). (2) Metabolic wastes: from all cells → to kidneys (excreted). (3) Heat: from all cells → to skin (radiated to environment).

q: What is a portal system in the cardiovascular context?
a: A portal system joins two capillary beds in series — blood passes through one capillary bed, then a portal vessel, then a second capillary bed before returning to the heart (e.g., hepatic portal system).

q: Define CAPILLARIES.
a: Capillaries are the smallest, most numerous blood vessels in the body — microscopic, thin-walled (a single layer of endothelial cells). They are the site of exchange: O2, nutrients, hormones, and wastes diffuse between the blood and surrounding tissue fluid across capillary walls.

q: Define ARTERIES.
a: Arteries are blood vessels that carry blood AWAY from the heart toward the tissues. They typically carry oxygenated blood (exception: pulmonary arteries, which carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs).

q: Define VEINS.
a: Veins are blood vessels that RETURN blood TO the heart from the tissues. They typically carry deoxygenated blood (exception: pulmonary veins, which carry oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the left atrium).

q: What is the SEPTUM of the heart and what does it divide?
a: The septum is a central muscular wall dividing the heart into LEFT and RIGHT halves, each functioning as an independent pump. Under normal conditions, blood from the two sides does not mix. The interventricular septum divides the two ventricles; the interatrial septum divides the two atria.

q: What are the ATRIA (singular: atrium)? What is their function?
a: The atria are the two UPPER, thin-walled chambers of the heart. They receive blood returning to the heart: the RIGHT ATRIUM receives deoxygenated blood from the body (via vena cavae); the LEFT ATRIUM receives oxygenated blood from the lungs (via pulmonary veins). They pump blood downward into the ventricles.

q: What are the VENTRICLES? What is their function and how do left and right differ?
a: The ventricles are the two LOWER, thick-walled chambers that generate the primary pumping force of the heart. RIGHT VENTRICLE: pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs via the pulmonary trunk (low-pressure pump). LEFT VENTRICLE: pumps oxygenated blood to the entire body via the aorta (high-pressure pump, with thicker walls).

q: What are the PULMONARY ARTERIES? What do they carry and where?
a: The pulmonary arteries carry DEOXYGENATED blood from the RIGHT VENTRICLE to the LUNGS. They are the only arteries in the body that carry deoxygenated blood. The pulmonary trunk exits the right ventricle and splits into left and right pulmonary arteries supplying each lung.

q: What are the PULMONARY VEINS? What do they carry and where?
a: The pulmonary veins carry OXYGENATED blood from the LUNGS back to the LEFT ATRIUM. They are the only veins in the body that carry oxygenated blood. There are four pulmonary veins (two from each lung).

q: What is the AORTA? Where does it originate and what does it carry?
a: The aorta is the LARGEST ARTERY in the body. It originates from the LEFT VENTRICLE (via the aortic valve) and carries oxygenated blood into the systemic circulation. It is both the starting point of systemic circulation and the starting/ending reference point of the overall cardiovascular circuit.

q: What is the SUPERIOR VENA CAVA? What body region does it drain and where does it deliver blood?
a: The superior vena cava (SVC) is a large vein that collects and returns deoxygenated blood from the UPPER BODY (head, neck, arms, upper thorax) to the RIGHT ATRIUM.

q: What is the INFERIOR VENA CAVA? What body region does it drain and where does it deliver blood?
a: The inferior vena cava (IVC) is the largest vein in the body. It collects and returns deoxygenated blood from the LOWER BODY (abdomen, pelvis, lower limbs) to the RIGHT ATRIUM.

q: What is the PULMONARY TRUNK? Where does it originate and what does it become?
a: The pulmonary trunk is the large arterial vessel that exits from the RIGHT VENTRICLE (via the pulmonary valve) and carries deoxygenated blood toward the lungs. It bifurcates (divides) into the LEFT and RIGHT PULMONARY ARTERIES, each supplying one lung.

q: Define PULMONARY CIRCULATION.
a: Pulmonary circulation is the circuit carrying blood from the RIGHT VENTRICLE through the LUNGS and back to the LEFT ATRIUM. It is a low-pressure, short circuit dedicated to gas exchange (CO2 out, O2 in). Path: right ventricle → pulmonary trunk → pulmonary arteries → lung capillaries → pulmonary veins → left atrium.

q: Define SYSTEMIC CIRCULATION.
a: Systemic circulation is the circuit carrying blood from the LEFT VENTRICLE through ALL BODY TISSUES (except lungs) and back to the RIGHT ATRIUM. It is a high-pressure, long circuit delivering O2 and nutrients and removing wastes. Path: left ventricle → aorta → systemic arteries → capillaries → veins → superior/inferior vena cava → right atrium.

q: Trace the complete flow of blood starting and ending at the AORTA, passing through both circulations.
a: Aorta → systemic arteries → systemic capillaries (O2/nutrient delivery; CO2/waste pickup) → systemic veins → superior/inferior vena cava → RIGHT ATRIUM → right ventricle → pulmonary trunk → pulmonary arteries → lung capillaries (CO2 released; O2 absorbed) → pulmonary veins → LEFT ATRIUM → left ventricle → AORTA.

q: Define the PERICARDIUM. What is the function of pericardial fluid?
a: The pericardium (peri = around + kardia = heart) is a tough, fibrous membranous sac that completely encloses the heart. Pericardial fluid fills the space between the pericardium and the heart's outer surface, lubricating the heart as it beats and reducing friction.

q: Define the MYOCARDIUM. What is it composed of and which chamber has the thickest myocardium?
a: The myocardium (myo = muscle + kardia = heart) is the muscular wall of the heart, composed of cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes). It forms the bulk of the heart. The LEFT VENTRICLE has the thickest myocardial wall because it must generate high pressure to pump blood throughout the entire systemic circulation.

q: What is the CORONARY CIRCULATION? What does 'coronary' mean?
a: The coronary circulation is the dedicated blood supply for the HEART MUSCLE ITSELF. 'Coronary' derives from corona (= crown) because the coronary vessels encircle the heart near its base like a crown. The heart cannot rely on diffusion from blood inside its chambers — it has its own arterial supply.

q: Where do the CORONARY ARTERIES originate? Name the two main coronary arteries and the general region each supplies.
a: Both coronary arteries originate at the ROOT OF THE AORTA, just above (superior to) the aortic valve leaflets. (1) RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY (RCA): supplies right atrium, right ventricle, and inferior left ventricle. (2) LEFT CORONARY ARTERY (LCA): branches into circumflex artery and anterior interventricular artery/LAD (most of left ventricle, interventricular septum).

q: What are CORONARY VEINS? How do they collect venous blood from the heart?
a: Coronary veins drain deoxygenated blood from the capillary beds within the myocardium. They run alongside the coronary arteries. The majority of coronary venous blood empties into the CORONARY SINUS, while a small amount drains directly into the right atrium via smaller anterior cardiac veins.

q: What is the CORONARY SINUS? Where is it located and where does it drain?
a: The coronary sinus is a venous reservoir (large venous channel) located on the POSTERIOR surface of the heart. It collects approximately 85% of coronary venous blood from the cardiac veins and drains directly into the RIGHT ATRIUM.

q: In what direction does blood flow, and why?
a: Blood flows from regions of HIGHER pressure to regions of LOWER pressure. The contraction of the heart creates the pressure gradient that drives this flow.

q: What is the mean blood pressure in the aorta, and what is it in the venae cavae?
a: Aorta: approximately 93 mm Hg (highest). Venae cavae: a few mm Hg (lowest). Blood flows down this pressure gradient from aorta through arteries, arterioles, capillaries, venules, veins, to venae cavae.

q: What is a pressure gradient (deltaP) and what does it determine?
a: A pressure gradient (deltaP) is the difference in pressure between two regions (P1 - P2). Flow is directly proportional to deltaP: the higher the pressure gradient, the greater the fluid flow (Flow proportional to deltaP).

q: Does blood flow depend on absolute pressure or on the pressure GRADIENT?
a: Blood flow depends on the PRESSURE GRADIENT (deltaP), NOT on absolute pressure. Two tubes with different absolute pressures but equal deltaP will have equal flow.

q: What is hydrostatic pressure?
a: Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by a fluid even if it is NOT in motion. It is exerted in all directions.

q: What is driving pressure?
a: Driving pressure is the pressure created by the contraction of the heart that pushes blood out into vessels. Contraction creates pressure WITHOUT changing the volume of blood.

q: State Poiseuille's Law and its formula for resistance.
a: Poiseuille's Law: R = 8Ln / (pi x r^4), or R proportional to Ln/r^4. Resistance increases with tube LENGTH (L) and fluid VISCOSITY (n); resistance decreases dramatically as tube RADIUS (r) increases (inversely proportional to the 4th power of radius).

q: According to Poiseuille's Law, how does radius affect resistance? Give a numerical example.
a: Resistance is inversely proportional to radius to the FOURTH power (R proportional to 1/r^4). Example: Tube A (radius=1) has Flow proportional to 1; Tube B (radius=2) has Flow proportional to 16. Doubling the radius increases flow 16-fold.

q: What is the combined flow relationship incorporating pressure gradient AND resistance?
a: Flow proportional to deltaP/R. Flow increases as pressure gradient increases (directly proportional) AND as resistance decreases (inversely proportional).

q: What is the formula for velocity of flow, and how does vessel cross-sectional area affect it?
a: Velocity (v) = Flow rate (Q) / Cross-sectional area (A). A NARROWER vessel has FASTER velocity for the same flow rate (e.g., area=1 cm^2 gives v=12 cm/min; area=12 cm^2 gives v=1 cm/min at same Q=12 cm^3/min).

q: What is Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) and what is its formula?
a: Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) is the primary driving force for blood flow — the pressure reserved in the arteries during heart relaxation. Formula: MAP = Cardiac Output (CO) x Peripheral Resistance (PR).

q: What are the 6 structural differences between cardiac muscle cells and skeletal muscle cells?
a: 1. Smaller and have a SINGLE nucleus per fiber. 2. Branch and join neighboring cells through intercalated disks. 3. Contain GAP JUNCTIONS. 4. T-tubules are LARGER and branch. 5. Sarcoplasmic reticulum is SMALLER. 6. Mitochondria occupy ONE-THIRD of cell volume (more than skeletal).

q: What is the functional significance of the SPIRAL arrangement of ventricular muscle?
a: The spiral arrangement of ventricular muscle allows ventricular contraction to SQUEEZE blood UPWARD from the apex of the heart toward the outflow tracts.

q: What are the TWO types of myocardial cells? State the primary function of each.
a: (1) CONTRACTILE CELLS: the working muscle cells of the heart; contain organized sarcomeres; generate the mechanical force of contraction. They make up the bulk of the heart. (2) AUTORHYTHMIC CELLS (pacemakers): specialized cells that spontaneously generate action potentials without external stimulation; set the rate and rhythm of the heartbeat. They make up ~1% of myocardial cells.

q: Describe the STRUCTURAL features of autorhythmic (pacemaker) cells that distinguish them from contractile cells.
a: Autorhythmic cells are SMALLER in size, contain FEW or NO organized sarcomeres, do NOT have well-developed T-tubule systems, and do NOT contribute significantly to contractile force. Their defining feature is an UNSTABLE membrane potential (pacemaker potential). They are located in specific conduction regions: SA node, AV node, bundle of His, and Purkinje fibers.

q: Describe the ARRANGEMENT of contractile myocardial cells in the heart and how they are electrically coupled.
a: Contractile cells are arranged in two branching networks (syncytia): an ATRIAL syncytium and a VENTRICULAR syncytium. Cells connect end-to-end at INTERCALATED DISKS containing (1) DESMOSOMES (mechanically couple adjacent cells, transferring contractile force) and (2) GAP JUNCTIONS (electrically couple cells, allowing depolarization to spread rapidly so all cells contract nearly simultaneously).

q: Explain the importance of the VALVES of the heart. What would happen without them?
a: Heart valves ensure ONE-WAY blood flow by opening passively when upstream pressure exceeds downstream pressure and closing when the pressure gradient reverses. Without valves: blood would flow BACKWARD (regurgitation) at every stage, recirculating rather than advancing through the circuit — the heart would waste enormous energy.

q: What are ATRIOVENTRICULAR (AV) VALVES? Name both, give their locations, and state their function.
a: AV valves are valves located BETWEEN the atria and ventricles. (1) TRICUSPID valve: between right atrium and right ventricle. (2) BICUSPID (mitral) valve: between left atrium and left ventricle. Function: prevent backflow of blood from ventricles back into atria during ventricular contraction (ensure one-way flow downward and out).

q: Describe the TRICUSPID VALVE: exact location, number of cusps, and memory aid.
a: The tricuspid valve is located between the RIGHT ATRIUM and RIGHT VENTRICLE. It has THREE cusps (flaps). Memory aid: R-S-T = Right Side Tricuspid.

q: Describe the BICUSPID (MITRAL) VALVE: exact location, number of cusps, and origin of the name 'mitral'.
a: The bicuspid valve (also called the MITRAL valve) is located between the LEFT ATRIUM and LEFT VENTRICLE. It has TWO cusps. The name 'mitral' comes from its resemblance to a bishop's MITER (tall, pointed headdress).

q: What are SEMILUNAR VALVES? Name both, give their locations, structure, and function.
a: Semilunar valves are located BETWEEN the ventricles and the great arteries. (1) AORTIC valve: between left ventricle and aorta. (2) PULMONARY valve: between right ventricle and pulmonary trunk. Each has THREE cuplike (crescent-shaped) leaflets. Function: prevent backflow from arteries into ventricles during ventricular relaxation.

q: Describe the AORTIC VALVE: type, location, when it opens, and when it closes.
a: The aortic valve is a SEMILUNAR valve located between the LEFT VENTRICLE and the AORTA. Opens when left ventricular pressure EXCEEDS aortic pressure (during ventricular ejection). Closes when aortic pressure drops below aortic pressure after ejection — preventing regurgitation.

q: Describe the PULMONARY VALVE: type, location, when it opens, and when it closes.
a: The pulmonary valve is a SEMILUNAR valve located between the RIGHT VENTRICLE and the PULMONARY TRUNK. Opens when right ventricular pressure EXCEEDS pulmonary arterial pressure. Closes when pulmonary arterial pressure exceeds right ventricular pressure during relaxation.

q: What are PAPILLARY MUSCLES and CHORDAE TENDINEAE? What is their function?
a: Papillary muscles are mound-shaped projections of the ventricular MYOCARDIUM. They connect via fibrous cords called CHORDAE TENDINEAE to the edges of the AV valve flaps. Function: they prevent the AV valve flaps from being pushed backward (prolapsing or everting) into the atria during the high-pressure ventricular contraction phase.

q: What is the 3-step mechanism of AV VALVE OPENING?
a: Step 1: Blood returning to the heart fills the atria, increasing atrial pressure, which FORCES AV VALVES OPEN. Step 2: As ventricles fill, AV valve flaps HANG LIMPLY into the ventricles. Step 3: Atria contract (atrial systole), FORCING ADDITIONAL BLOOD into the ventricles.

q: What is the 3-step mechanism of AV VALVE CLOSING?
a: Step 1: Ventricles contract, FORCING BLOOD UPWARD AGAINST AV VALVE CUSPS. Step 2: AV VALVES CLOSE (atrial pressure is now less than ventricular pressure). Step 3: Papillary muscles contract and chordae tendineae TIGHTEN, PREVENTING valve flaps from EVERTING into the atria.

q: What is the mechanism of SEMILUNAR VALVE OPENING?
a: As ventricles contract and INTRAVENTRICULAR PRESSURE RISES, blood is pushed upward against the semilunar valves. When ventricular pressure EXCEEDS arterial pressure, blood FORCES THE SEMILUNAR VALVES OPEN and ejection begins.

q: What is the mechanism of SEMILUNAR VALVE CLOSING?
a: As ventricles relax and INTRAVENTRICULAR PRESSURE FALLS below arterial pressure, blood flows BACK from arteries toward the heart, FILLING THE CUSPS of the semilunar valves and FORCING THEM CLOSED. This backflow produces the dicrotic notch on the aortic pressure tracing.

q: List all 10 steps of cardiac EC coupling.
a: 1. AP enters from adjacent cell. 2. Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open; Ca2+ enters cell. 3. Ca2+ induces Ca2+ release through RyR. 4. Local release causes Ca2+ spark. 5. Summed Ca2+ sparks create a Ca2+ signal. 6. Ca2+ binds troponin to initiate contraction. 7. Relaxation occurs when Ca2+ unbinds from troponin. 8. Ca2+ pumped back into SR (SERCA). 9. Ca2+ exchanged for Na+ by NCX antiporter. 10. Na+ gradient maintained by Na+-K+-ATPase.

q: What are voltage-gated L-type Ca2+ channels (DHP receptors) in cardiac muscle?
a: L-type Ca2+ channels (also called DHP receptors) are voltage-gated channels in the T-tubule membrane. They open during depolarization, allowing a SMALL initial influx of Ca2+ into the cytosol. This small Ca2+ spark then triggers the much larger Ca2+ release from the SR via RyR.

q: What is Ca2+-INDUCED Ca2+ RELEASE (CICR)? Which channel triggers it and where does it occur?
a: CICR is the cardiac EC coupling mechanism where a small amount of Ca2+ entering the cell from the ECF through voltage-gated Ca2+ channels (during the AP plateau) acts as a TRIGGER signal for RYANODINE RECEPTOR (RyR) channels on the SARCOPLASMIC RETICULUM (SR) to open, releasing a much LARGER amount of stored Ca2+ into the cytosol.

q: What are RYANODINE RECEPTOR (RyR) CHANNELS? Where are they located and what is their role in EC coupling?
a: RyR channels are Ca2+-release channels located on the SARCOPLASMIC RETICULUM (SR) membrane. In cardiac EC coupling, they are activated ('triggered') when Ca2+ entering from the ECF during the action potential plateau binds to them. Opening of RyR channels releases stored SR Ca2+ in large quantities into the cytosol (CICR mechanism).

q: What is the Na+-Ca2+ ANTIPORTER (NCX)? State its location, stoichiometry, energy source, and role in cardiac RELAXATION.
a: NCX is a secondary active transport protein in the CARDIAC CELL MEMBRANE. It removes Ca2+ from the cell during relaxation by moving 1 Ca2+ OUT in exchange for 3 Na2+ IN. The energy comes from the Na+ ELECTROCHEMICAL GRADIENT (maintained by Na+-K+-ATPase) — not directly from ATP. NCX lowers cytosolic Ca2+ after contraction.

q: What is the role of the SODIUM-POTASSIUM PUMP (Na+-K+-ATPase) in cardiac EC coupling?
a: Na+-K+-ATPase maintains the Na+ ELECTROCHEMICAL GRADIENT by actively pumping 3 Na+ OUT and 2 K+ IN per cycle (using ATP). This Na+ gradient is the energy source for NCX. If Na+-K+-ATPase is inhibited (e.g., by digitalis), the Na+ gradient diminishes → NCX activity decreases → cytosolic Ca2+ accumulates → stronger contractions.

q: Describe PHASE 4 of the cardiac CONTRACTILE cell action potential: ion channels, membrane potential value, and what makes it 'stable.'
a: Phase 4 is the STABLE RESTING MEMBRANE POTENTIAL at approximately -90 mV. It is maintained by K+ LEAK CHANNELS. Unlike autorhythmic cells, contractile cells do NOT spontaneously depolarize — Phase 4 remains flat until a depolarizing wave arrives from an adjacent cell via gap junctions.

q: Describe PHASE 0 (DEPOLARIZATION) of the cardiac contractile cell AP: trigger, ion/channel, and magnitude of voltage change.
a: Phase 0 — Rapid Depolarization: a wave of depolarization enters from an adjacent contractile cell via GAP JUNCTIONS. Voltage-gated Na+ CHANNELS open → massive rapid Na+ INFLUX → membrane potential shoots from -90 mV to approximately +20 mV. This is the fastest phase of the AP.

q: Describe PHASE 1 (INITIAL REPOLARIZATION) of the cardiac contractile cell AP.
a: Phase 1 — Initial Repolarization: voltage-gated Na+ channels CLOSE (fast inactivation). Brief opening of fast K+ channels → K+ EFFLUX → cell begins to repolarize slightly (small dip from +20 mV). This phase is BRIEF and quickly transitions into the plateau.

q: Describe PHASE 2 (PLATEAU) of the cardiac contractile cell AP. Which two opposing ion events maintain the plateau?
a: Phase 2 — Plateau: the membrane potential stabilizes near 0 mV for ~200 msec due to a BALANCE between two events: (1) DECREASED K+ efflux — fast K+ channels close. (2) INCREASED Ca2+ influx — voltage-gated Ca2+ channels (L-type) open slowly and allow steady Ca2+ inward current. Ca2+ influx during this phase triggers CICR.

q: Describe PHASE 3 (RAPID REPOLARIZATION) of the cardiac contractile cell AP.
a: Phase 3 — Rapid Repolarization: L-type Ca2+ channels CLOSE, eliminating the depolarizing Ca2+ current. Simultaneously, slow voltage-gated K+ channels OPEN → large K+ EFFLUX → membrane rapidly returns to -90 mV (Phase 4).

q: What is the REFRACTORY PERIOD in cardiac contractile cells? How long does it last relative to the AP, and why?
a: The refractory period is the time after an AP during which the cell CANNOT fire a second AP. In cardiac contractile cells it lasts almost as long as the entire AP (~200 msec) because voltage-gated Na+ channels remain INACTIVATED throughout the prolonged depolarization/plateau. They cannot re-open until the cell repolarizes fully.

q: Why can cardiac muscle NOT undergo SUMMATION?
a: Summation requires a second AP to arrive while the cell is still contracted from the first. This cannot occur in cardiac muscle because the REFRACTORY PERIOD lasts nearly as long as the mechanical twitch (~200 msec each). By the time the refractory period ends, the muscle has nearly fully RELAXED.

q: Why can cardiac muscle NOT undergo TETANUS, and why is this physiologically critical?
a: Tetanus = sustained contraction from summation. Since summation is impossible in cardiac muscle (due to the long refractory period), tetanus CANNOT occur. This is CRITICAL: a tetanic ventricle would NEVER RELAX, so it could never fill with blood between beats — cardiac output would collapse.

q: What is the PACEMAKER POTENTIAL? How does it differ from a resting membrane potential?
a: The pacemaker potential is the UNSTABLE, spontaneously drifting membrane potential of autorhythmic cells. Unlike a resting MP (stable, fixed), the pacemaker potential starts at approximately -60 mV AFTER each repolarization and automatically drifts UPWARD toward threshold due to If channel activity. This cycle repeats automatically.

q: What are IF CHANNELS? At what membrane potential do they open, what ions flow through them, and what is the net result?
a: If channels (funny current channels) open at HYPERPOLARIZED (negative) potentials — approximately -60 mV — in autorhythmic cells. They are permeable to BOTH Na+ and K+, but at these potentials Na+ INFLUX exceeds K+ EFFLUX → net inward current → SLOW DEPOLARIZATION of the cell.

q: Why are If channels called FUNNY CURRENT (If) channels?
a: If channels were called 'funny' because their behavior was OPPOSITE to all other known voltage-gated channels at the time of discovery. Most voltage-gated channels open when the cell DEPOLARIZES; If channels open when the cell HYPERPOLARIZES (at negative potentials).

q: What does HCN stand for in HCN channels? Decode each term.
a: HCN = Hyperpolarization-Activated Cyclic Nucleotide-Gated channels. H = Hyperpolarization-activated (open at negative potentials). C = Cyclic Nucleotide-Gated (activity modulated by cyclic nucleotides such as cAMP). N = (channelName). These are the ion channels underlying the pacemaker potential (If/funny current).

q: What is the threshold of autorhythmic cells and what event triggers the action potential?
a: Threshold of autorhythmic cells: approximately -40 mV. Near threshold, SOME Ca2+ channels begin to open, reinforcing depolarization while If channels close. When -40 mV is reached, MANY Ca2+ channels open rapidly, triggering the action potential.

q: What is Phase 0 of the AUTORHYTHMIC cell action potential, and why is it different from contractile cells?
a: Phase 0 (depolarization): LOTS of Ca2+ channels open, causing a slower upstroke. Na+ channels are ABSENT in autorhythmic cells, so depolarization is carried entirely by Ca2+ (slower and smaller amplitude than contractile cell Phase 0).

q: What is Phase 3 of the AUTORHYTHMIC cell action potential?
a: Phase 3 (repolarization): K+ channels OPEN, K+ efflux repolarizes the cell back toward -60 mV. Ca2+ channels CLOSE as the potential returns to -60 mV. At -60 mV, If channels open again and the next pacemaker potential cycle begins.

q: Describe the COMPLETE sequence of ion channel events in the autorhythmic cell pacemaker potential and action potential.
a: 1. After repolarization at -60 mV: IF (HCN) channels OPEN → net Na+ influx → SLOW depolarization (pacemaker potential). 2. As potential rises: If channels gradually close; one set of Ca2+ channels opens. 3. At THRESHOLD: different voltage-gated Ca2+ channels (L-type) open → rapid Ca2+ influx → STEEP DEPOLARIZATION. 4. REPOLARIZATION: Ca2+ channels close; slow K+ channels open → K+ efflux → return to -60 mV → cycle repeats.

q: Why can PACEMAKER cells initiate action potentials WITHOUT innervation from the nervous system?
a: Pacemaker cells have an UNSTABLE membrane potential that spontaneously drifts upward toward threshold after each repolarization, driven by IF CHANNELS. When the pacemaker potential reaches threshold, Ca2+ channels open and fire an AP — no external signal needed. The nervous system can MODULATE rate but is not required to initiate it.

q: How do action potentials SPREAD from autorhythmic cells to contractile cells?
a: Autorhythmic cells SPONTANEOUSLY fire action potentials. Depolarizations spread RAPIDLY to adjacent contractile cells through GAP JUNCTIONS located at INTERCALATED DISKS. Electrical current flows directly between cells, making cardiac muscle function as an electrical syncytium.

q: Table 14.3 — Compare the RESTING MEMBRANE POTENTIAL of contractile myocardial cells vs. autorhythmic myocardial cells.
a: Contractile cells: STABLE resting MP at approximately -90 mV (set by K+ leak channels). Autorhythmic cells: NO true stable resting MP — instead have an UNSTABLE PACEMAKER POTENTIAL starting at approximately -60 mV after each repolarization.

q: Table 14.3 — Compare the RISING PHASE (depolarization mechanism) of contractile vs. autorhythmic myocardial cell APs.
a: Contractile cells: rising phase driven by rapid Na+ INFLUX through voltage-gated Na+ channels. Autorhythmic cells: rising phase driven by Ca2+ INFLUX through voltage-gated Ca2+ channels (NOT Na+ channels) — this is why blockers of Na+ channels do not eliminate the autorhythmic cell AP.

q: Table 14.3 — Compare the AP DURATION of contractile vs. autorhythmic myocardial cells.
a: Contractile cells: LONG AP duration of 200+ msec due to the Ca2+ plateau phase (Phase 2). Autorhythmic cells: AP duration approximately 150+ msec. Both cell types have AP durations far longer than neurons or skeletal muscle cells (~1-5 msec).

q: Table 14.3 — Compare the significance of the REFRACTORY PERIOD in contractile vs. autorhythmic myocardial cells.
a: Contractile cells: LONG, physiologically critical refractory period lasting nearly as long as the mechanical twitch — prevents summation and tetanus. Autorhythmic cells: have a refractory period, but it is NOT the rate-limiting factor; the duration of the spontaneous pacemaker potential cycle determines how fast the cell fires.

q: Table 14.3: What events lead to THRESHOLD in each of the three muscle types?
a: Skeletal muscle: Net Na+ entry through ACh-operated (nicotinic) channels at neuromuscular junction. Contractile myocardium: Depolarization ENTERS via gap junctions from adjacent cells. Autorhythmic myocardium: Net Na+ entry through If channels; REINFORCED by Ca2+ entry near threshold.

q: Table 14.3: Does HYPERPOLARIZATION occur in contractile myocardium after repolarization?
a: NO hyperpolarization in contractile myocardium. The resting potential is -90 mV, which is at (or near) the equilibrium potential for K+, so no overshoot occurs.

q: What is the SINOATRIAL (SA) NODE? Location, intrinsic rate, and role.
a: The SA node is a small cluster of autorhythmic cells located in the POSTERIOR WALL of the RIGHT ATRIUM. Intrinsic rate: ~70 bpm (fastest of all pacemakers). Role: DOMINANT PACEMAKER of the heart — its APs spread to all other cardiac cells first, so the SA node determines heart rate under normal conditions.

q: What is the INTERNODAL PATHWAY? What two events does signal conduction through it accomplish?
a: The internodal pathway is a network of specialized conducting fibers running through the atrial walls from the SA NODE to the AV NODE. Conduction accomplishes two things simultaneously: (1) delivers the AP to the AV node for transmission to the ventricles, and (2) spreads depolarization across the ATRIAL CONTRACTILE CELLS, causing atrial contraction.

q: What is the ATRIOVENTRICULAR (AV) NODE? Location, backup rate, and two key functions.
a: The AV node is a cluster of autorhythmic cells at the FLOOR of the RIGHT ATRIUM near the interventricular septum. Backup pacemaker rate: ~50 bpm. Two functions: (1) Acts as the ONLY ELECTRICAL PATHWAY from atria to ventricles (fibrous skeleton insulates elsewhere). (2) Produces AV NODE DELAY — slows transmission so atria complete contraction before ventricular systole begins.

q: What is the AV BUNDLE (Bundle of His)? Location and function.
a: The AV bundle (bundle of His) is a bundle of conducting fibers that originates at the AV node and travels through the INTERVENTRICULAR SEPTUM, carrying the AP from the atria into the ventricular myocardium. It divides into left and right BUNDLE BRANCHES.

q: What are the BUNDLE BRANCHES? Where do they travel and what do they connect?
a: The left and right bundle branches are continuations of the AV bundle that run down EACH SIDE of the interventricular septum toward the APEX of the heart. They carry the AP signal from the AV bundle to the PURKINJE FIBERS at the ventricular apex.

q: What are PURKINJE FIBERS? Location, conduction speed, and backup pacemaker rate.
a: Purkinje fibers are large, specialized conducting cells that branch throughout the walls of the VENTRICLES from the apex outward. Conduction speed: up to 4 m/sec (the fastest in the heart) — ensures nearly simultaneous contraction of all ventricular cells. Backup pacemaker rate: ~25-40 bpm.

q: What is AV NODE DELAY? How slow is AV conduction relative to the internodal pathway, and what is its physiological purpose?
a: AV node delay is the DELIBERATE SLOWING of AP conduction through the AV node. Physiological purpose: ensures ATRIA COMPLETE THEIR CONTRACTION and blood finishes entering the ventricles BEFORE ventricular systole begins, maximizing EDV and therefore stroke volume.

q: Describe the complete 5-step CONDUCTION SEQUENCE through the heart.
a: 1. SA NODE fires spontaneously. 2. AP travels via internodal pathways → AV node; atria contract. 3. AV NODE DELAY slows the signal. 4. AP passes through AV BUNDLE → LEFT/RIGHT BUNDLE BRANCHES → PURKINJE FIBERS at the APEX. 5. Depolarization sweeps UPWARD from apex through ventricular contractile cells → ventricles contract from bottom up.

q: Define DIASTOLE and SYSTOLE.
a: DIASTOLE (Greek: diastole = dilation): the period during which cardiac muscle RELAXES; chambers fill with blood. SYSTOLE (Greek: systole = contraction): the period during which cardiac muscle CONTRACTS; blood is ejected. Atria and ventricles have separate systole and diastole events.

q: What are the FIVE phases of the cardiac cycle (names only)?
a: 1. Late diastole. 2. Atrial systole. 3. Isovolumic ventricular contraction. 4. Ventricular ejection. 5. Isovolumic ventricular relaxation.

q: Describe LATE DIASTOLE (Phase 1 of the cardiac cycle): what is happening, valve status, and approximate percentage of ventricular filling accomplished.
a: Late Diastole: both atria and ventricles are RELAXED. Blood flows PASSIVELY by gravity/venous pressure from veins through open atria into ventricles. AV valves: OPEN. Semilunar valves: CLOSED. Approximately 70-80% of ventricular filling occurs PASSIVELY during this phase before any muscle contraction.

q: Describe ATRIAL SYSTOLE (Phase 2 of the cardiac cycle): what happens, valve status, and define END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (EDV).
a: Atrial Systole: atria CONTRACT, actively pumping the remaining ~20% of blood into the ventricles (the 'atrial kick'). AV valves: OPEN. Semilunar valves: CLOSED. END of atrial systole = maximum blood volume in the ventricle = END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (EDV). Normal resting EDV: approximately 135 mL.

q: Define END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (EDV). When exactly does it occur in the cardiac cycle, and what is the normal resting value?
a: EDV = the MAXIMUM volume of blood in the ventricle, measured at the END of ventricular filling (end of atrial systole / start of ventricular contraction). It represents the preload. Normal resting EDV: approximately 135 mL.

q: Describe ISOVOLUMIC VENTRICULAR CONTRACTION (Phase 3 of the cardiac cycle): valve status, pressure change, volume change.
a: Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction: ventricles BEGIN contracting; rising ventricular pressure pushes AV valves CLOSED (S1 sound). Semilunar valves REMAIN CLOSED (ventricular pressure not yet high enough). VOLUME DOES NOT CHANGE (iso = same, volumic = volume) — blood has nowhere to go, so pressure RISES rapidly.

q: Why is Phase 3 called 'isovolumic'?
a: 'Isovolumic' means the volume DOES NOT CHANGE. During Phase 3, all four heart valves are CLOSED. Ventricular pressure is rising due to muscle contraction, but it has not yet exceeded arterial pressure, so semilunar valves cannot open. Volume stays constant at EDV.

q: Describe VENTRICULAR EJECTION (Phase 4 of the cardiac cycle): valve status, blood flow, and define END-SYSTOLIC VOLUME (ESV).
a: Ventricular Ejection: ventricular pressure rises above arterial pressure → SEMILUNAR VALVES OPEN → blood ejected into arteries. AV valves remain CLOSED; atria begin refilling passively. The residual volume remaining when ejection ends = END-SYSTOLIC VOLUME (ESV). Normal resting ESV: approximately 65 mL.

q: Define END-SYSTOLIC VOLUME (ESV). When does it occur and what is the normal resting value?
a: ESV = the MINIMUM volume of blood remaining in the ventricle at the END of ventricular ejection (end of systole), just as the semilunar valves close. It is the blood NOT ejected. Normal resting ESV: approximately 65 mL. ESV = EDV - SV.

q: Describe ISOVOLUMIC VENTRICULAR RELAXATION (Phase 5 of the cardiac cycle): valve status, pressure change, volume change.
a: Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation: ventricles RELAX; ventricular pressure falls. When ventricular pressure drops below ARTERIAL pressure → blood flows backward briefly → SEMILUNAR VALVES SNAP CLOSED (S2 sound). Both AV and semilunar valves are CLOSED; volume does NOT change. When ventricular pressure finally drops below ATRIAL pressure → AV valves open → cycle restarts.

q: Define VENTRICULAR SYSTOLE. Which two phases of the cardiac cycle does it encompass?
a: Ventricular systole = the period during which the ventricles CONTRACT. It encompasses: Phase 3 (Isovolumic Ventricular Contraction) and Phase 4 (Ventricular Ejection). It begins at S1 (AV valve closure) and ends at S2 (semilunar valve closure).

q: Define VENTRICULAR DIASTOLE. Which two phases of the cardiac cycle does it encompass?
a: Ventricular diastole = the period during which the ventricles RELAX and fill. It encompasses: Phase 5 (Isovolumic Ventricular Relaxation) and Phase 1 (Late Diastole/ventricular filling). It begins at S2 (semilunar valve closure) and ends at S1 of the next cycle.

q: Explain the PRESSURE CHANGES during the cardiac cycle and how they govern flow through the heart and vessels.
a: Blood flows from HIGH to LOW pressure. Valves open and close PASSIVELY in response to pressure gradients. When ventricular pressure EXCEEDS arterial pressure → semilunar valves open. When ventricular pressure FALLS BELOW arterial pressure → semilunar valves close. When ventricular pressure FALLS BELOW atrial pressure → AV valves open.

q: What is the dicrotic notch on the aortic pressure tracing?
a: The dicrotic notch is a brief DOWNWARD NOTCH visible in the aortic pressure tracing. It corresponds to the CLOSURE of the AORTIC SEMILUNAR VALVE (producing S2). It represents the momentary backflow of blood into the aortic valve cusps before they snap shut.

q: Describe the 4 panels of the Wiggers Diagram (what each panel shows).
a: Panel 1 (top): ECG (P wave, QRS, T wave). Panel 2: Pressure (mm Hg) — shows aortic pressure, left ventricular pressure, and left atrial pressure with labeled events (S1, S2, dicrotic notch). Panel 3: Heart sounds (S1 at start of systole, S2 at end of systole). Panel 4 (bottom): Left ventricular volume (mL) — rises to EDV (~135 mL), drops to ESV (~65 mL).

q: On the Pressure-Volume loop, identify what happens at each labeled point: A, A', B, C, and D.
a: A (START): ~65 mL, low pressure (ESV; AV valve opens). A to A': Ventricular FILLING (diastole); volume rises to ~135 mL. B (EDV, ~135 mL): AV valve closes; isovolumic contraction begins; pressure rises. C: Semilunar valves OPEN; ejection begins. D (ESV, ~65 mL): Semilunar valves CLOSE; isovolumic relaxation begins. D back to A: Pressure falls at constant volume.

q: What is the FIRST HEART SOUND (S1)? What causes it, when does it occur, and what does it mark?
a: S1 ('lub') = the first audible heart sound caused by the CLOSURE OF THE AV VALVES (tricuspid and mitral) at the START of isovolumic ventricular contraction (Phase 3). It marks the ONSET OF VENTRICULAR SYSTOLE.

q: What is the SECOND HEART SOUND (S2)? What causes it, when does it occur, and what does it mark?
a: S2 ('dup') = the second audible heart sound caused by the CLOSURE OF THE SEMILUNAR VALVES (aortic and pulmonary valves) at the START of isovolumic ventricular relaxation (Phase 5). It marks the ONSET OF VENTRICULAR DIASTOLE.

q: What is the sequence of heart sounds during a NORMAL cardiac cycle?
a: S1 ('Lub') — S2 ('Dup') — [silence] — S1 ('Lub')... Normal blood flow is LAMINAR (smooth, non-turbulent) and therefore SILENT between the two valve sounds.

q: What is a heart MURMUR and what causes it?
a: A heart murmur is an ABNORMAL heart sound. Normal blood flow is LAMINAR (smooth, streamlined) and SILENT. A murmur occurs when blood flow becomes TURBULENT — due to a structural valve problem — causing vibrations audible with a stethoscope.

q: What is an INCOMPETENT (insufficient/leaky) valve, and what sound does it produce?
a: An incompetent valve FAILS TO CLOSE COMPLETELY (insufficient seal). Result: TURBULENT BACKFLOW (regurgitation) through the leaky valve. Sound: A SWISHING sound. Also called an insufficient or leaky valve.

q: What is a STENOTIC valve, and what sound does it produce?
a: A stenotic valve is STIFFENED AND NARROWED, failing to open fully. Result: TURBULENT FLOW through the narrowed opening. Sound: A HIGH-PITCHED WHISTLE or a CLICK. The narrowed orifice forces blood through at high velocity.

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – SWISH – Dup': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-SWISH-Dup = SYSTOLIC murmur between S1 and S2. Indicates: INSUFFICIENT (leaky) AV valve (mitral or tricuspid). During systole, when the AV valve should be closed, blood leaks backward through the incompetent valve, causing turbulent backflow (the swish).

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – Dup – SWISH': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-Dup-SWISH = DIASTOLIC murmur after S2. Indicates: INSUFFICIENT (leaky) SEMILUNAR valve (pulmonary or aortic). During diastole, when the semilunar valve should be closed, blood leaks backward from the artery into the ventricle (the swish).

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – WHISTLE – Dup': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-WHISTLE-Dup = SYSTOLIC murmur between S1 and S2. Indicates: STENOTIC SEMILUNAR valve (pulmonary or aortic). During systole, blood is forced through a narrowed semilunar valve opening, producing a high-pitched whistle.

q: Murmur pattern 'Lub – Dup – WHISTLE': what does this indicate?
a: Lub-Dup-WHISTLE = DIASTOLIC murmur after S2. Indicates: STENOTIC AV valve (mitral or tricuspid stenosis). During diastole, when blood is filling the ventricle through the AV valve, the narrowed AV valve opening forces blood through at high velocity, producing the whistle.

q: Compare normal valve sounds vs. abnormal valve sounds (4-panel summary).
a: Normal OPEN valve: laminar flow = QUIET. Normal CLOSED valve: no flow = QUIET. Stenotic (narrowed) valve: turbulent flow through narrow opening = MURMUR. Insufficient (leaky) valve: turbulent backflow = MURMUR.

q: How does an ECG DIFFER from a single-cell action potential recording?
a: ECG: records SUMMED electrical activity of ALL cardiac cells from BODY SURFACE electrodes — smaller amplitude (~1 mV), can detect both depolarization and repolarization events as separate waves. Single-cell AP: intracellular recording — much LARGER amplitude (~110 mV), recorded directly at the source. The ECG is NOT simply a single AP.

q: What does the P WAVE of an ECG represent?
a: The P wave represents ATRIAL DEPOLARIZATION — the spread of electrical activity across both atria from the SA node. Atrial contraction begins during the LATTER PART of the P wave.

q: What does the P-R SEGMENT of an ECG represent? What event occurs during this flat line?
a: The P-R segment is the isoelectric (flat) baseline between the P wave and the QRS complex. It represents the AV NODE DELAY — the slowed conduction of the AP through the AV node, AV bundle, and bundle branches. During this time, atrial contraction is completing and the ventricles are still filling.

q: What is the PR INTERVAL (as distinct from the P-R segment)? What does a prolonged PR interval suggest clinically?
a: The PR interval = P WAVE + P-R SEGMENT combined; measured from the start of the P wave to the start of the QRS complex. It represents the total time from atrial depolarization onset to ventricular depolarization onset. A PROLONGED PR interval (>200 msec) suggests slowed AV node conduction (HEART BLOCK).

q: What do the individual Q, R, and S WAVES represent within the QRS complex?
a: Q wave: first DOWNWARD deflection — initial ventricular depolarization (interventricular septum). R wave: first UPWARD (positive) deflection — main ventricular depolarization wave. S wave: DOWNWARD deflection after the R wave — final depolarization of basal ventricular regions.

q: What does the QRS COMPLEX as a whole represent? What other event is hidden within it?
a: The QRS complex represents progressive VENTRICULAR DEPOLARIZATION — the AP sweeping through the ventricular contractile myocardium from apex to base. ATRIAL REPOLARIZATION also occurs during this time but its small electrical signal is MASKED by the larger QRS complex.

q: What does the T WAVE represent? Why is there NO separate wave for atrial repolarization?
a: The T wave represents VENTRICULAR REPOLARIZATION — the return of ventricular cells to resting state (start of ventricular relaxation). Atrial repolarization has NO separate wave because it is electrically BURIED/MASKED by the larger ventricular signal (QRS complex).

q: What does the S-T SEGMENT of an ECG represent? What is its clinical significance?
a: The S-T segment is the flat baseline between the end of the QRS complex and the start of the T wave. It represents EARLY VENTRICULAR REPOLARIZATION (corresponding to the plateau phase of the ventricular AP). CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT: S-T ELEVATION suggests acute MI (STEMI); S-T DEPRESSION suggests ischemia.

q: What is the QT INTERVAL? What does it measure and what is its clinical significance?
a: QT interval = QRS complex + S-T segment + T wave combined. It represents the TOTAL DURATION of ventricular electrical activity (depolarization + repolarization). CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT: a PROLONGED QT interval indicates delayed ventricular repolarization → risk of dangerous ventricular arrhythmias.

q: What does the T-P SEGMENT of the ECG represent?
a: The T-P segment represents VENTRICULAR AND ATRIAL RELAXATION. It is the isoelectric (baseline) period between the end of the T wave and the beginning of the next P wave.

q: Match each ECG event to its corresponding MECHANICAL event in the cardiac cycle.
a: P wave → atrial DEPOLARIZATION → atrial contraction begins. P-R segment → AV node delay → atrial contraction finishes. QRS complex → ventricular DEPOLARIZATION → ventricular contraction begins (S1). S-T segment → ventricular AP plateau → isovolumic contraction/early ejection. T wave → ventricular REPOLARIZATION → ventricular relaxation begins (S2).

q: What is the standard ECG paper scale?
a: Horizontal: 5 mm = one small box; 25 mm = 1 second. Vertical: +1 millivolt at top of y-axis. This allows calculation of heart rate and measurement of interval durations.

q: What are the three ECG deflection rules based on current flow direction relative to electrodes?
a: Rule 1: UPWARD deflection = current vector moving TOWARD the positive electrode. Rule 2: DOWNWARD deflection = current vector moving toward the NEGATIVE electrode. Rule 3: NO deflection (baseline) = current vector is PERPENDICULAR to the electrode axis.

q: What is an ECG LEAD and what are the three configurations of Einthoven's Triangle (Leads I, II, III)?
a: A lead is a specific VIEWPOINT of cardiac electrical activity, recording the voltage difference between two electrodes. Lead I: Right arm (-) to Left arm (+). Lead II: Right arm (-) to Left leg (+). Lead III: Left arm (-) to Left leg (+). Together these form Einthoven's Triangle.

q: What does a 12-lead ECG provide vs. a 3-lead ECG?
a: A 12-lead ECG uses 12 different electrode viewpoints to provide a THREE-DIMENSIONAL view of cardiac electrical activity, allowing more precise localization of arrhythmias and infarcts. The classic system uses 3 leads (Einthoven's Triangle).

q: What are the 4 questions to ask when interpreting an ECG tracing?
a: 1. What is the RATE? Is it within the normal range of 60-100 bpm? 2. Is the RHYTHM regular or irregular? 3. Are all normal WAVES present in recognizable form? 4. Is there ONE QRS complex for each P wave? If yes, is the P-R segment CONSTANT in length?

q: Define TACHYCARDIA, BRADYCARDIA, and normal SINUS RHYTHM (with bpm values).
a: Sinus rhythm (normal): 60-100 bpm. Tachycardia: heart rate FASTER than normal (greater than 100 bpm). Bradycardia: heart rate SLOWER than normal (less than 60 bpm). Heart rate is measured as the time between two consecutive P waves or R waves.

q: What is THIRD-DEGREE BLOCK and what does its ECG look like?
a: Third-degree block (complete heart block): The AV node FAILS to conduct any impulses from atria to ventricles. The atria and ventricles beat INDEPENDENTLY at different rates. ECG shows P waves (atrial rate) and R waves (ventricular rate) that are DISSOCIATED — more P waves than QRS complexes.

q: What is ATRIAL FIBRILLATION and what does its ECG look like?
a: Atrial fibrillation: CHAOTIC, disorganized atrial electrical activity. ECG shows: irregular rapid small oscillations (no distinct P waves), irregular R-R intervals (irregular ventricular response). The atria quiver rather than contract in a coordinated fashion.

q: What is VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION and what does its ECG look like?
a: Ventricular fibrillation: CHAOTIC, disorganized ventricular electrical activity — a life-threatening emergency. ECG shows: completely chaotic irregular waveforms with NO identifiable P, QRS, or T waves. Results in cardiac output of zero.

q: Define STROKE VOLUME (SV). Write the formula and state normal resting values for EDV, ESV, and SV.
a: Stroke volume (SV) = the volume of blood pumped by ONE VENTRICLE during ONE CONTRACTION. Formula: SV = EDV - ESV. Normal resting values: EDV = 135 mL; ESV = 65 mL; SV = 135 - 65 = 70 mL/beat.

q: Define EJECTION FRACTION (EF). Write the formula and state the normal resting value. What EF value suggests heart failure?
a: Ejection fraction (EF) = the PERCENTAGE of EDV pumped out with each contraction. Formula: EF = (SV / EDV) x 100%. Normal resting value: (70 mL / 135 mL) x 100% = approximately 52%. An EF below 40% is the clinical threshold for HEART FAILURE WITH REDUCED EJECTION FRACTION (HFrEF).

q: Define CARDIAC OUTPUT (CO). Write the formula and calculate the normal resting value.
a: Cardiac output (CO) = the total volume of blood pumped by ONE VENTRICLE per unit time. Formula: CO = HR x SV. Normal resting value: 72 bpm x 70 mL/beat = approximately 5,040 mL/min (~5 L/min). Both ventricles must maintain EQUAL CO under steady-state conditions.

q: CALCULATION: Given HR = 80 bpm and SV = 75 mL/beat, calculate cardiac output (CO).
a: CO = HR x SV = 80 bpm x 75 mL/beat = 6,000 mL/min (6.0 L/min).

q: CALCULATION: Given EDV = 160 mL and ESV = 55 mL, calculate SV, ejection fraction (EF), and CO if HR = 70 bpm.
CALCULATION: If ejection fraction = 30% and EDV = 200 mL, calculate SV and ESV. If HR = 90 bpm, calculate CO. What does this EF suggest clinically?
a: SV = EDV x EF = 200 mL x 0.30 = 60 mL. ESV = EDV - SV = 200 - 60 = 140 mL. CO = 90 x 60 = 5,400 mL/min. EF of 30% is below the clinical threshold of 40% — indicates HEART FAILURE WITH REDUCED EJECTION FRACTION (HFrEF).

q: How do changes in HEART RATE, EDV, and ESV each independently affect cardiac output?
a: HEART RATE: CO = HR x SV; if HR doubles and SV stays constant, CO doubles. EDV INCREASES (more venous return): larger EDV → more stretch → Frank-Starling effect → larger SV → higher CO. ESV DECREASES (increased contractility): more blood squeezed out → larger SV = EDV - ESV → higher CO.

q: What factors affect HEART RATE (HR) as a determinant of cardiac output?
a: HR is determined by the RATE OF DEPOLARIZATION in autorhythmic cells. It is DECREASED by parasympathetic innervation (ACh on M receptors). It is INCREASED by sympathetic innervation (NE on B1 receptors) and epinephrine.

q: What factors affect STROKE VOLUME (SV) as a determinant of cardiac output?
a: SV is determined by FORCE of contraction and is influenced by: 1. CONTRACTILITY (increased by sympathetic/epinephrine via cAMP/Ca2+). 2. END-DIASTOLIC VOLUME (preload) — determined by venous return, which is aided by skeletal muscle pump, respiratory pump, and venous constriction.

q: Summarize AUTONOMIC NERVOUS SYSTEM control of heart rate: which divisions are involved, what do they do, and which dominates at rest?
a: Both ANS divisions innervate the SA node. PARASYMPATHETIC (vagus nerve): SLOWS heart rate. SYMPATHETIC: SPEEDS UP heart rate. At REST: PARASYMPATHETIC DOMINATES — continuous parasympathetic activity slows the SA node's intrinsic rate (~90-100 bpm) down to the normal resting rate (~70 bpm).

q: Describe the PARASYMPATHETIC mechanism of heart rate control at the CELLULAR level.
a: Parasympathetic neurons release ACh → binds MUSCARINIC cholinergic receptors on SA node cells → two effects: (1) K+ PERMEABILITY INCREASES → cell HYPERPOLARIZES. (2) Ca2+ PERMEABILITY DECREASES → slower rate of spontaneous depolarization. Combined: pacemaker potential takes LONGER to reach threshold → SLOWER heart rate.

q: Describe the SYMPATHETIC mechanism of heart rate control at the CELLULAR level.
a: Sympathetic neurons release NE (and adrenal medulla releases epinephrine) → binds BETA-1 (β1) adrenergic receptors on SA node cells → activates ADENYLYL CYCLASE → increases cAMP → PROTEIN KINASE A activation → increases Na+ and Ca2+ influx through If (HCN) and Ca2+ channels → FASTER rate of depolarization → HIGHER heart rate.

q: What is PHOSPHOLAMBAN? Describe its role in the UNPHOSPHORYLATED vs. PHOSPHORYLATED state.
a: Phospholamban is a regulatory protein associated with the SR Ca2+-ATPase (SERCA) pump. UNPHOSPHORYLATED (baseline): INHIBITS SERCA, slowing Ca2+ reuptake into SR → slower relaxation. PHOSPHORYLATED (catecholamines → β1 → cAMP → PKA): inhibition RELIEVED → SERCA activity increases → more Ca2+ pumped into SR → stronger contraction AND faster relaxation.

q: What are the two outcomes of catecholamine-induced phosphorylation on cardiac contraction?
a: Outcome 1: MORE FORCEFUL CONTRACTION — increased Ca2+ stores in SR lead to greater Ca2+ release and stronger actin-myosin interaction. Outcome 2: SHORTER DURATION of contraction — Ca2+ is removed from the cytosol faster (shortens Ca2+-troponin binding time), allowing quicker relaxation.

q: What is CONTRACTILITY?
a: Contractility is the INTRINSIC ABILITY of a cardiac muscle fiber to CONTRACT at any given fiber length. It reflects the force of contraction independent of preload (fiber stretch). Increased contractility produces a greater stroke volume at the same EDV.

q: What is an INOTROPIC AGENT and give examples of positive and negative inotropes.
a: An inotropic agent is any chemical that affects CONTRACTILITY. POSITIVE inotropes (increase contractility): Epinephrine, Norepinephrine (catecholamines), Digitalis. NEGATIVE inotropes (decrease contractility): e.g., certain beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers.

q: What is DIGITALIS and how does it function as a cardiac drug?
a: Digitalis is a POSITIVE INOTROPE — it INCREASES cardiac contractility. It works by inhibiting the Na+/K+-ATPase pump, raising intracellular Na+, which then reduces Ca2+ efflux via the NCX exchanger, resulting in MORE Ca2+ available for contraction (greater force).

q: What is the FRANK-STARLING LAW of the heart?
a: Frank-Starling Law: Stroke volume is PROPORTIONAL to EDV. Greater ventricular stretch (preload) at the end of diastole leads to a MORE FORCEFUL contraction and therefore a GREATER stroke volume. In essence: the heart pumps all the blood that is returned to it.

q: On the Starling Curve, what are the normal RESTING VALUES for EDV and SV?
a: Normal resting values (Starling curve): EDV approximately 135 mL; Stroke Volume approximately 70 mL. Cardiac muscle normally operates on the ASCENDING LIMB of its length-tension curve.

q: What is PRELOAD, what determines it, and which clinical conditions INCREASE it?
a: Preload: degree of ventricular STRETCH at the beginning of contraction, determined by EDV (which is determined by venous return). Conditions that INCREASE preload: 1. Hypervolemia (increased blood volume). 2. Regurgitation of cardiac valves (backflow). 3. Heart failure.

q: What is AFTERLOAD, what determines it, and which clinical conditions INCREASE it?
a: Afterload: the combined load of EDV and ARTERIAL RESISTANCE that the ventricle must overcome during contraction. Determined by: EDV and arterial blood pressure. Conditions that INCREASE afterload: 1. Hypertension. 2. Vasoconstriction. Effect: Increased afterload = increased cardiac workload.

q: What are the three main contributors to VENOUS RETURN?
a: 1. SKELETAL MUSCLE PUMP: skeletal muscle contractions during exercise squeeze veins, pushing blood toward the heart. 2. RESPIRATORY PUMP: pressure changes during breathing (especially inspiration) lower thoracic pressure, drawing blood toward the heart. 3. VENOUS CONSTRICTION: sympathetic stimulation constricts veins, reducing their capacity and increasing venous return.

q: What cardiac output factors are influenced by SYMPATHETIC stimulation / epinephrine?
a: Sympathetic/epinephrine increases: 1. HEART RATE (via increased Na+ and Ca2+ influx in pacemaker cells). 2. CONTRACTILITY (via B1 receptors and cAMP/phospholamban pathway). 3. VENOUS RETURN (via venous constriction, increasing EDV/preload).

q: What are the four functional roles of the major vessel types in the cardiovascular system? (Ch. 15 preview)
a: 1. ELASTIC SYSTEMIC ARTERIES: pressure reservoir that maintains blood flow during ventricular relaxation. 2. ARTERIOLES: site of variable resistance (adjustable diameter controls blood distribution). 3. CAPILLARIES: site of exchange between blood and cells. 4. SYSTEMIC VEINS: expandable volume reservoir (low-pressure reservoir).

q: END
a: END
